Maḥmúd-i-Muqári’í, a noted cloth dealer. He was newly married and had attained the presence of the Báb in the castle of C̱hihríq.
“I am too much fired,” he replied, “with the love of this Cause to be able to devote any attention to my son. I am impatient to join Quddús and to enlist under his banner.”

Siyyid Muḥammad-Riḍáy-i-Pá-Qal‘iyí, a distinguished siyyid and a highly esteemed divine, whose declared purpose to enlist under the banner of Mullá Ḥusayn caused a great tumult among the ‘ulamás of Iṣfahán.

canonical_name: the siyyid who walked on foot from Ḵhurásán to Bárfurús̱h
db_statements: The siyyid who walked on foot all the way from Ḵhurásán to Bárfurús̱h, where he fell a victim to the bullet of the enemy.

canonical_name: Siyyid Aḥmad, father of Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-‘Azíz
db_statements: Siyyid Aḥmad, the father of Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-‘Azíz, the amanuensis of the Báb

canonical_name: Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, son of Siyyid Aḥmad
db_statements: Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, son of Siyyid Aḥmad, whose head was blown off by the ball from a cannon as he was standing at the entrance of the fort, and who, because of his tender age, was greatly loved and admired by Quddús.
S̱hayḵh ‘Alí, son of S̱hayḵh ‘Abdu’l-Ḵháliq-i-Yazdí, a resident of Mas̱hhad, a youth whose enthusiasm and untiring energy were greatly praised by Mullá Ḥusayn and Quddús.

Mírzá Muḥammad-‘Alí, a noted divine, whose father, Ḥájí Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb, was one of the most distinguished mujtahids in Qazvín. He attained the presence of the Báb in S̱híráz, and was enrolled as one of the Letters of the Living.
his father, Ḥájí Mullá ‘Abdu’l-Vahháb, was one of the most distinguished mujtahids in Qazvín.

canonical_name: Muḥammad-Hádí, son of Ḥájí ‘Abdu’l-Karím, surnamed Bág̱hbán-Bás̱hí

Siyyid Aḥmad,
Mírzá ‘Abdu’l-Jalíl, a noted divine
Mírzá Mihdí.
From the village of Lahárd, a man named Ḥájí Muḥammad-‘Alí, who had greatly suffered as a result of the murder of Mullá Taqí in Qazvín.
the murder of Mullá Taqí in Qazvín.
Mullá Mihdí, a distinguished divine, who had been one of the esteemed disciples of Siyyid Káẓim. He was noted for his learning, his eloquence, and his staunchness of faith.
Mullá Maḥmúd-i-Ḵhu’í, brother of Mullá Mihdí, one of the Letters of the Living and a distinguished divine.
Mullá Yúsuf-i-Ardibílí, one of the Letters of the Living, noted for his learning, his enthusiasm and eloquence. It was he who had aroused the apprehensions of Ḥájí Karím Ḵhán on his arrival at Kirmán
On his arrival in Mázindarán, he was made captive by the forces of Prince Mihdí-Qulí Mírzá
Mullá Jalíl-i-Urúmí, one of the Letters of the Living, noted for his learning, his eloquence, and tenacity of faith.
Mullá Aḥmad, a resident of Marág̱hih, one of the Letters of the Living, and a distinguished disciple of Siyyid Káẓim.

canonical_name: Mullá Báqir, brother of Mullá Mihdíy-i-Kandí
db_statements: Mullá Báqir, brother of Mullá Mihdí, both of whom were men of considerable learning, to whose great attainments Bahá’u’lláh testifies in the “Kitáb-i-Íqán.”

Siyyid Káẓim, a resident of Zanján, and one of its noted merchants. He attained the presence of the Báb in S̱híráz, and accompanied Him to Iṣfahán. His brother, Siyyid Murtaḍá, was one of the Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán.
His brother, Siyyid Murtaḍá, was one of the Seven Martyrs of Ṭihrán.
Iskandar, also a resident of Zanján, who, together with Ḥasan and Qulí, bore the body of Mullá Ḥusayn to the fort.
together with Ḥasan and Qulí, bore the body of Mullá Ḥusayn to the fort.

all residents of Zanján.

‘Abdu’l-Muḥammad,

Siyyid Aḥmad — all residents of Zanján.
Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-Kuláh-Dúz, a resident of Bárfurús̱h, whose head was impaled on a lance and was paraded through its streets.

The last three were masons by profession, were natives of Kirmán, and resided in Qáyin in the province of Ḵhurásán.

canonical_name: Ustád Qásim, son of Ustád Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín
db_statements: Ustád Qásim, son of Ustád Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín
Ustád ‘Alí-Akbar, brother of Ustád Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín.
Mullá Riḍáy-i-S̱háh and a young man from Bahnimír were slain two days after the abandonment of the fort by Quddús, in the Panj-S̱hanbih-Bázár of Bárfurús̱h.
a young man from Bahnimír were slain two days after the abandonment of the fort by Quddús, in the Panj-S̱hanbih-Bázár of Bárfurús̱h.

Ḥájí Mullá Muḥammad-i-Ḥamzih, surnamed the S̱harí‘at-Madár, succeeded in burying their bodies in the neighbourhood of the Masjid-i-Káẓim-Big, and in inducing their murderer to repent and ask forgiveness.

Mullá Muḥammad-i-Mu‘allim-i-Núrí, an intimate companion of Bahá’u’lláh who was closely associated with Him in Núr, in Ṭihrán, and in Mázindarán. He was famed for his intelligence and learning, and was subjected, Quddús only excepted, to the severest atrocities that have ever befallen a defender of the fort of Ṭabarsí.
“Never will I consent,” he replied, “to vilify the beloved of God at the bidding of a man such as you.”

Ḥájí Muḥammad-i-Karrádí, whose home was situated in one of the palm groves adjoining the old city of Bag̱hdád, a man of great courage who had fought and led a hundred men in the war against Ibráhím Pás̱há of Egypt. ... He was seventy-five years old when he embraced the Faith of the Báb
a man of great courage who had fought and led a hundred men in the war against Ibráhím Pás̱há of Egypt.

Sa‘íd-i-Jabbáví, a native of Bag̱hdád, who displayed extraordinary courage during the siege. He was shot in the abdomen, and, though severely wounded, managed to walk until he reached the presence of Quddús. He joyously threw himself at his feet and expired.

The circumstances of the martyrdom of these last two companions were related by Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib-i-Sang-Sárí, one of those who survived that memorable siege, in a communication he addressed to Bahá’u’lláh.
I recalled the counsels of my father, Mír Muḥammad-‘Alí ... reached the fort, met Mullá Ḥusayn, and determined to consecrate the remaining days of my life to the service of the Cause

canonical_name: Siyyid Aḥmad, brother of Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib-i-Sang-Sárí
db_statements: his two brothers, Siyyid Aḥmad and Mír Abu’l-Qásim, both of whom were martyred while defending the fort.

canonical_name: Mír Abu’l-Qásim, brother of Siyyid Abú-Ṭálib-i-Sang-Sárí
db_statements: his two brothers, Siyyid Aḥmad and Mír Abu’l-Qásim, both of whom were martyred while defending the fort.

“On the day on which Ḵhusraw was slain,” he wrote, “I happened to be the guest of a certain Karbilá’í ‘Alí-Ján ... He had gone to assist in the protection of Ḵhusraw, and had returned and was relating to me the circumstances attending his death.
I happened to be the guest of a certain Karbilá’í ‘Alí-Ján, the kad-ḵhudá of one of the villages in the neighbourhood of the fort.
I recalled the counsels of my father, Mír Muḥammad-‘Alí, who exhorted me to arise and help in the promotion of the Cause of the Báb.

canonical_name: G̱hulám ‘Alí, son of Ḥusayn-i-‘Arab (father of Nabíl)
db_statements: My father belonged to the tribe of Ṭáhirí, who led a nomadic life in the province of Ḵhurásán. His name was G̱hulám ‘Alí, son of Ḥusayn-i-‘Arab.
His name was G̱hulám ‘Alí, son of Ḥusayn-i-‘Arab.
He married the daughter of Kalb-‘Alí, and by her had three sons and three daughters.
a certain Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-Zavári’í arrived at Zarand and was able to enlighten me on a subject which had become the ruling passion of my life.
The story which this Siyyid Ḥusayn-i-Zavári’í related to me served to allay my agitation.

I too, unable to remain in Zavárih, departed for Kás̱hán, in which town I spent the winter and met Ḥájí Mírzá Jání, of whom my cousin had spoken, and who gave me a treatise written by the Báb, entitled ‘Risáliy-i-‘Adlíyyih,’

‘My name is Ḥájí Zaynu’l-‘Ábidín. I intended not to separate myself from Him, but He bade me remain in this place and convey to any of His friends whom I might meet His loving greetings, and dissuade them from following Him.’
He advised me, however, to remain in Qum together with a certain Mírzá Fatḥu’lláh-i-Ḥakkák, a lad of my age whom he had recently guided to the Cause

My friend Mírzá Fatḥu’lláh subsequently followed me. He was eventually arrested and shared the fate of those who were put to death in the year 1268 A.H. as a result of the attempt on the life of the S̱háh.
One day I unexpectedly came across my maternal uncle, Naw-Rúz-‘Alí, who had come on purpose to fetch me.

canonical_name: the defenders of the Fort of S̱hayḵh Ṭabarsí
the mujtahids and ‘ulamás (Muslim divines of Persia)

db_statements: My father belonged to the tribe of Ṭáhirí, who led a nomadic life in the province of Ḵhurásán.

canonical_name: the visiting Tablet for Mullá Ḥusayn
db_statements: The first page He wrote was dedicated to the memory of Mullá Ḥusayn. In the visiting Tablet revealed in his honour, He extolled, in moving terms, the unswerving fidelity with which he served Quddús throughout the siege of the fort of Ṭabarsí.

canonical_name: the visiting Tablets in memory of the martyrs of Ṭabarsí
db_statements: entrusted to his care the visiting Tablets He had revealed in memory of the martyrs of Ṭabarsí, and bade him perform, on His behalf, a pilgrimage to that spot.

He was eventually released, as we have already observed, by the companions of Mullá Ḥusayn on the day of the battle of Vás-Kas.
I was born on the eighteenth of Ṣafar in the year 1247 A.H., in the village of Zarand.
From Rubát-Karím I returned to Zarand.

I was in the village of Rubát-Karím, on a visit to my maternal uncle, when, on the twelfth day after Naw-Rúz, in the year 1263 A.H., I accidentally overheard, in the masjid of that village, a conversation between two men which first made me acquainted with the Revelation of the Báb.
I watched him as he prayed at the Masjid-i-Imám-Ḥasan and performed, with scrupulous care and extreme piety, all the rites and ceremonies prescribed by his Faith.
Arriving in Ṭihrán, I proceeded directly to the Masjid-i-S̱háh, which was opposite a madrisih
